content:
3 min read
Just now

--

A zero-budget experiment with Inna Story and the slow, messy work of building a digital entity that Google and AI systems can actually recognize.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

In May I put out my first song under the name Inna Story. No money for promotion, no big plan – just decided to release it and see what happens if I treat the whole thing as a real test of how digital identities actually form these days.
A few weeks later the track showed up at the very top of Yandex Alice results for the exact words from the title. That was surprising, because there are older songs with the same name that have been around for years. It wasn’t magic. It came from paying attention to how the project gets described and connected across different places online.
The biggest headache from the beginning was the name. “Inna Story” gets mixed up with the famous Romanian singer INNA all the time, and also with regular Instagram stories. Algorithms don’t like ambiguity. They need clear signals to understand this is a separate thing.
What helped was being very direct about it. On the official site I wrote plainly what Inna Story is and what it isn’t. I started using the full name “Inna Story (Inna Udalaya)” everywhere instead of hoping context would do the job. I also made sure the project was properly linked to stable records – ORCID, ISNI, MusicBrainz, Discogs – so there are fixed points that don’t move.
Right now I’m working on making the same thing clearer for Google. That means adding proper structured data on the website and creating an entry in Wikidata. At the same time I’m slowly moving some of the writing into English. The idea is simple: if the entity is described consistently in more places, it becomes easier for different systems to trust it.
The whole process feels slower than I expected. Small changes in how you describe something don’t show results immediately. They accumulate. Some things only become visible weeks later. It’s not dramatic work, but it seems to matter.
I’m still figuring a lot of this out as I go. The second song is almost ready, English texts are just starting, and I’m trying to be honest about what’s actually moving the needle and what’s just noise.
If you’re also trying to make something (music, a project, your own name) more visible and understandable to AI tools and search engines right now, I’d be curious what part feels the most unclear to you.
FAQ
What is Inna Story exactly?
It’s the name I use for my music. Cinematic, atmospheric pop. At the same time I use the project to test how independent artists can build clearer digital identities in 2026.
Why do you keep writing the full name “Inna Story (Inna Udalaya)”?
Because short versions get confused with other people and things. Adding the real name and being consistent helps systems separate one entity from another.
Did the Yandex result happen because of SEO tricks?
No paid promotion and no tricks. It came from making the basic information about the project consistent and easy to find across platforms.
What are you doing for Google right now?
Adding schema.org markup on the official site and setting up a Wikidata page. These are the concrete technical steps that help Google understand who or what something is.
Where can people listen to the music?
The first single is on all major streaming platforms. Everything is collected on innastoryofficial.com.
AEO, GEO, Digital Identity, Knowledge Graph, Independent Music, Personal Project, AI, Music Experiment, Zero Budget, Building in Public
